transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Karen. I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi. Thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been feeling a bit overlooked lately. It’s just that sometimes it seems like my contributions don’t really matter, you know? I mean, I help out when asked, but it feels like I’m not really recognized for what I bring to the table.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Thank you for sharing that, Karen. It sounds like you're feeling unappreciated for your contributions, and that can be really tough. Can you tell me more about the situations where you feel overlooked? What specific moments come to mind?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, there was this one time when my coworker got a lot of praise for his work, and then everyone turned to me to help him with some tech issues. I just felt... I don’t know, like my own skills and input were just ignored. It’s like I’m just there to support others, but no one really sees what I can do. It’s frustrating, I guess.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds really frustrating, Karen. It seems like in that moment, your own skills and contributions were overshadowed by your coworker’s recognition, and that left you feeling sidelined. I can imagine how that would make you question your value in the team. How did that experience affect you afterwards?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it did affect me. After that, I found myself just going through the motions, helping out reluctantly. I kept thinking about how I felt underutilized, and it just made me feel... sad, I guess. I started ruminating on it, which didn’t help. I mean, I want to be a team player, but it’s hard when you feel like you’re not really part of the team in a meaningful way.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like that experience really impacted your motivation and emotional well-being. Feeling sad and underutilized can create a heavy weight, especially when you want to contribute meaningfully. I hear that you're struggling with the balance between wanting to support your team and feeling like your own contributions aren’t valued. How do you usually cope with those feelings when they come up?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I suppose I just try to push through it. I help out when I’m asked, but inside, I’m often just... thinking about how I wish things were different. I don’t really talk about it much, though. It feels easier to just keep my head down and do what’s needed rather than bring it up. I guess I worry that if I say something, it might just make things awkward or worse.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re managing a lot internally while trying to maintain a sense of normalcy at work. Keeping your head down and pushing through can feel like a safer option, but it also seems to leave you feeling stuck and unfulfilled. I can understand why you might worry about bringing it up with others. What do you think holds you back the most from expressing how you feel?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it’s a mix of things. Part of me worries that if I speak up, people might think I’m just complaining or being difficult. I don’t want to create tension or make anyone uncomfortable. Plus, there’s this nagging thought that maybe I’m just being overly sensitive about it. It’s like I don’t want to rock the boat, but at the same time, I feel like I’m just floating along without really being seen.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That’s a really insightful reflection, Karen. It seems like you’re caught between wanting to be honest about your feelings and the fear of being perceived as difficult or overly sensitive. It’s understandable to want to avoid tension, especially in a work environment. Yet, it also sounds like you’re yearning for a sense of visibility and recognition. How do you think it would feel to find a way to express your thoughts without rocking the boat?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it would feel... relieving, maybe? Like, just being able to share what I’m experiencing without feeling judged. I mean, I’d like to think that my colleagues would understand if I approached it the right way. But there’s still that fear of how it might be received. It’s hard to imagine a way to do it that wouldn’t feel awkward or put me in a vulnerable position. I guess I just want to feel heard without the risk of making things worse.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like the idea of sharing your feelings brings a sense of relief, but there's also a lot of apprehension about vulnerability and how it might be received. Wanting to be heard without risking discomfort is completely valid. It’s a delicate balance, and it makes sense that you’d want to approach it thoughtfully. What do you think might help you feel more comfortable or prepared if you were to share your feelings with your colleagues?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe if I had a clearer idea of what I wanted to say, it would help. Like, if I could frame it in a way that focuses on wanting to contribute more effectively rather than just expressing frustration. I think practicing what I want to say could make it feel less daunting. Also, having a supportive colleague or friend to talk to beforehand might give me a bit of confidence. But even with that, I still feel a bit hesitant about how it would go over.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like having a clear message and practicing it could really empower you, which is a great strategy. Framing it in a positive way, focusing on your desire to contribute more effectively, could help shift the conversation. Having a supportive colleague or friend to talk to beforehand also seems like it could provide some reassurance. It’s completely normal to feel hesitant, especially when it comes to sharing personal feelings in a work setting. What do you think would be the first step for you in preparing to have that conversation?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think the first step might be to jot down my thoughts. Just writing out what I want to say could help me organize my feelings and figure out how to express them. I could also think about specific examples of when I felt overlooked, so I can explain my perspective better. Once I have that down, maybe I could reach out to someone I trust to run it by them. That way, I can get some feedback and feel a bit more ready before actually talking to my colleagues.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a solid plan, Karen. Writing down your thoughts and organizing your feelings can really clarify what you want to express. Having specific examples will likely help illustrate your perspective, making it easier for others to understand where you’re coming from. Reaching out to someone you trust for feedback can also provide that extra layer of support and confidence. It seems like you’re taking thoughtful steps toward feeling more empowered in this situation. How do you feel about starting that process?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit more hopeful about it, actually. Just talking through it here has made it seem more manageable. I know it won’t be easy, but having a plan makes it feel less overwhelming. I guess I just need to take that first step and start writing things down. It feels like a small step, but it could lead to something better. I appreciate you helping me think this through.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad to hear that you’re feeling more hopeful, Karen. It’s amazing how just talking things through can help clarify your thoughts and make a situation feel more manageable. Taking that first step, even if it feels small, can lead to meaningful change. Remember, it’s okay to take your time with this process. I’m here to support you as you move forward. Is there anything else on your mind that you’d like to explore today?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you, I really appreciate that. I think I’ve shared a lot already, and I feel like I have a clearer path forward now. I guess I just want to make sure I follow through on this and don’t let my fears hold me back again. It’s nice to have someone to talk to about it. I’ll definitely keep working on my thoughts and maybe touch base again later when I’ve made some progress.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You're very welcome, Karen. It’s great to hear that you feel clearer about your path forward, and I admire your commitment to following through despite your fears. Having a plan can really help in staying accountable to yourself. I’d be more than happy to touch base again when you’re ready to share your progress or explore anything else. Remember, it’s a process, and you’re taking important steps. Take care, and I look forward to hearing from you again!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I appreciate your support and understanding. I’ll definitely keep working on this and reach out when I’m ready. It feels good to have a plan and someone to talk to. Take care, and I’ll see you next time!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You're very welcome, Karen! I'm glad to have been able to support you today. It’s wonderful to hear that you feel good about your plan. Take your time, and I look forward to hearing from you when you’re ready. Take care, and see you next time!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I’ll take care and work on my plan. Looking forward to our next conversation. See you!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You're welcome, Karen! I'm glad to hear that. Take care, and I look forward to our next conversation as well. See you soon!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: See you soon! Take care!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Take care, Karen! See you soon!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Take care! Looking forward to it!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Looking forward to it too, Karen! Take care!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thanks! You too!
